messages.content: So you're saying this internet legislation debate isn't just tech vs content companies fighting over money?

Exactly. People frame it as some insider battle between rich industries, but it's really about preserving the freedom to innovate. We're talking about services that hundreds of millions of people depend on - Facebook has 800 million users. This legislation threatens the entire foundation of how the internet works.
But how does it actually threaten innovation? What's the mechanism here?

It comes down to cost and uncertainty. Right now you can start a company incredibly cheaply - Foursquare reached 100,000 users on just $25,000 because of open source software and the internet as a distribution platform. But with this legislation, every startup will need a lawyer from day one because the definitions are so vague that no one can be sure they're compliant.
Wouldn't that affect big companies like Google too though?

That's the irony - Google already has 200 lawyers on staff. They can handle this. But a small search competitor like DuckDuckGo with three people? If their fourth hire has to be a lawyer instead of an engineer, they'll never compete. The legislation actually helps entrench the big players while killing innovation from startups.
Ok but why should regular people care about this beyond cheaper services?

Because we need internet innovation to solve massive problems coming our way. The economy is shifting to a modern platform and people in their 40s will need to retrain, be more self-reliant. The old model of government-run retraining programs is phenomenally expensive. But we're seeing education startups where peers teach peers at a fraction of the cost.
So you're saying the internet itself is the solution to economic disruption?

Precisely. The internet isn't the problem - it's how we dig ourselves out of these intractable challenges. We're going through economic dislocation whether we like it or not. The question is whether we'll have the innovative tools and platforms to help people adapt, or whether we'll cripple that innovation right when we need it most.
But what if the legislation gets refined to be less vague? Couldn't that solve the startup problem?

The fundamental issue is that any legislation in this space creates liability uncertainty that investors have to price in. As an investor, the first question I'll ask any startup is 'are you clean?' - what's your liability risk? Even with refinements, you're adding legal complexity and cost to every new venture. That chills investment and slows the pace of innovation when we can least afford it.
So you think we're basically in 1993 for crypto? That seems like a stretch given how much money has flowed in

Look, I know it sounds crazy with all the hype and money, but the infrastructure is still incredibly immature. Everything you can do with crypto today, you can do better with a regular database - except for one thing: distributing trust. We're still figuring out the basics.
But why does distributing trust matter so much? Seems like most people are fine with centralized systems

It's not about monetary policy or replacing government - it's about enabling much greater participation at the edges of innovation. Think about the mid-90s when there were 2,500 PC software companies making word processors and spreadsheets. By the end of the 90s, there was basically one. Centralization kills innovation at the margins.
Okay but how does crypto actually solve that consolidation problem? We're seeing the same thing happen with crypto - a few big exchanges, a few dominant chains

Fair point - we're definitely seeing some consolidation in the infrastructure layer. But the key difference is the underlying protocols remain open. Anyone can build on top of these chains without permission. It's like the difference between building on Windows vs building on the open web. The openness creates emergent, decentralized, bottom-up innovation that centralized systems just can't match.
You mentioned market forces dethroned Microsoft and IBM rather than antitrust. Think the same will happen to the big tech companies today?

That's the hope, but I'm honestly not sure. The network effects with Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon are incredibly strong - stronger than what Microsoft had. We've seen them copy innovations pixel by pixel when startups threaten them. The venue hasn't shifted enough yet. Crypto could be that shift, but we need the infrastructure to mature first.
What would it take for crypto to actually become that new venue? Seems like UX is still terrible

Exactly - the UX is awful, the infrastructure is slow and expensive, and honestly most applications don't need decentralization yet. But remember, the early web was pretty terrible too. We need better layer 2 solutions, better tooling, and applications that actually leverage the unique properties of decentralization. When that clicks, it could create the same kind of venue shift that moved us from desktop to web.
So as a VC, are you still investing heavily in crypto despite all these limitations?

We're being selective. The technology cycles matter enormously in venture - there are times of great opportunity and times when it's really tough. Right now consumer web services are incredibly difficult because of those dominant network effects I mentioned. Crypto represents a potential way out of that, but you have to be early and you have to be patient. Most of what we're seeing won't work, but the stuff that does could be transformational.
So you're saying blockchain is basically just a distributed ledger right now? That seems pretty underwhelming for all the hype

Look, I'll be honest - what it does now is very little. Mostly just securing Bitcoin as a store of value. But here's the thing - the internet was also a belief system at first. This is about believing in decentralized, bottom-up innovation and human creativity when given the tools.
But isn't Bitcoin just a ponzi scheme? Early adopters get rich, later ones get screwed?

That's the harshest critique and I get it. Early participants did better, just like any ponzi. But here's the key difference - with a real ponzi, you know the music will stop and there's no money left. These networks actually seem to create value, so later participants can still benefit. We just don't know the answer yet.
Okay but why should I care about this now? What's the urgency?

Because we're living through massive consolidation. Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon are dominating everything because they have more data, which creates better user experiences, which gets them more data. It's an accelerating circle. And when AI really hits, we'll have two choices - AI developed in their proprietary data stores, or AI in open public data stores.
So blockchain is supposed to be the alternative? How is that better than what we have?

Blockchains are radically open - public data stores distributed across many servers, identical copies everywhere. Anyone can join the network, download the entire history, see all the protocols. And if you don't like the direction? Fork it, take it somewhere else. Compare that to Facebook's proprietary algorithms creating your news feed.
But realistically, won't regular people just end up using whatever interface is easiest? Seems like we'd still get gatekeepers

That's exactly the risk. The technical gap is real. But what's exciting is this has mobilized people and capital around open source like never before. Companies that would have been proprietary are going open source. When 90% of startups fail and lose all their IP, this model lets you fork and evolve the failures.
You mentioned something about individuals controlling their own data stores. How would that actually work?

Each person maintains their own open data store and invites algorithms from different providers to operate on it. You get to decide if you like the output. You choose your algorithms instead of having them chosen for you. That's real autonomy instead of living in someone else's algorithmic bubble.
Sounds like a lot of work for the average person. Do you really think people will want to manage all that?

Fair point. But consider the alternative - we're already seeing what happens when proprietary algorithms construct our reality. If we don't figure out how to open this up and make it accessible, we won't be autonomous actors anymore. We'll just be products being optimized by systems we can't see or control.

source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Breakout Panel - Next Money_ New Tech for New Finance.m4a_spaced.txt

messages.content: Can you explain what SOPA is actually trying to accomplish? I keep hearing it's about piracy but the details are fuzzy

Sure - SOPA is basically trying to go after offshore pirate sites that host copyrighted content. The problem is that domestically we have the DMCA which works pretty well - if you notify a platform about infringing content, they take it down. But that doesn't reach sites operating from other countries where US law has no jurisdiction.
So why not just go after those foreign sites directly?

That's exactly the issue - content creators can't reach them because they're in jurisdictions where US law has no effect. So instead of going after the actual pirates, SOPA tries to make them invisible to US users by forcing intermediaries - search engines, ISPs, payment processors, social networks - to police their users and block access to these sites.
That sounds like a huge burden on those companies. Is that really fair?

Exactly my point. It's like making the phone company responsible for any crimes committed over their network. The phone company isn't committing crimes - they're just providing infrastructure. Same with internet services. This would create massive new liability for platforms built on user-generated content like YouTube and Facebook. They'd have to constantly monitor and police their users.
But isn't piracy actually a real problem that needs solving?

Of course piracy is a problem, but I think a lot of it stems from content simply not being available legally. I ran into this myself with a BBC show - the second season was right there on the BBC's own website, but they blocked me because I wasn't in the UK. The content industry carved up distribution rights in this complicated web of geographic and time windows that made sense when distribution was expensive, but now everything's global and instant.
So you think the content industry just needs to adapt their business model?

That's exactly it. The fundamental tension here is between the internet culture of empowering individuals and creating open access, versus the content industry's desire to control distribution. They grew up in an era of expensive production and distribution, so they parceled out rights very carefully. But now we live in a world where everything is accessible globally, and they're struggling to adapt.
But wouldn't changing those licensing models be incredibly complex given all the existing contracts and stakeholders?

Sure, it's complex, but I think they'll have to figure it out eventually. It doesn't make sense to criminalize your most passionate fans. People are willing to pay - I pay for Netflix, I would have paid the BBC for that content if they'd let me. The industry needs to find ways to serve people in the medium that allows inexpensive access to content, rather than trying to force the internet to conform to their old licensing model.
Do you think SOPA will actually pass and work as intended?

I think it's the wrong approach entirely. Even if it passes, it places this enormous burden on internet intermediaries without really solving the underlying problem. The real solution is for the content industry to adapt to global, instant distribution rather than trying to recreate artificial scarcity through legislation. The internet is fundamentally about empowering the edge and creating opportunity - SOPA goes against that grain.

source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Brad Burnham_ The Right to Piracy_ The Conflict Behind SOPA.mp4_spaced_spaced.txt

messages.content: So you're saying networks are fundamentally disrupting traditional business models - but isn't this just the usual creative destruction that's always happened in capitalism?

It's different this time because of the economics involved. Networks can bootstrap with almost nothing - Foursquare hit 100k users on less than $25k. The combination of open source software, internet infrastructure, and viral distribution creates unprecedented advantages for startups over established hierarchies.
But hierarchies served us well for over a century. Why can't they just adapt?

Hierarchies are structurally ill-equipped for this kind of innovation. Information has to flow up through layers, decisions flow back down - it's inherently slow. Plus organizations naturally protect their existing structures. When David Karp at Tumblr wants to test a feature, he hides it and lets it spread organically. Try doing that in a traditional corporation.
OK so if networks are so superior, why are you worried about policy? Shouldn't market forces just let the best model win?

Because incumbents aren't playing fair. They're using their existing relationships with policymakers to change the rules. Look at what happened in NYC - the hotel lobby got temporary rentals banned under the guise of "consumer safety" but it was really about killing Airbnb. Same pattern everywhere - telecom companies targeting Skype, publishers trying to block open access journals.
But some of these regulations might actually protect consumers though? How do we know when it's legitimate vs protectionism?

Ask who's really asking for the protection. It's not consumers demanding these restrictions - it's the incumbents. The Research Works Act wasn't researchers asking for help, it was publishers trying to slow down competition. When you see legislation affecting the open internet or competition, look at who's sponsoring it and whether it actually serves consumers or just protects existing business models.
You mentioned entrepreneurs are already thinking about moving operations offshore. Isn't that a bit dramatic?

After MegaUpload and JotForm got taken down, every single one of our portfolio companies had to reconsider where they store user data. Hundreds of thousands of people lost access to completely legitimate content they had stored. Our companies are now wondering if they should move to DNS servers outside the US. Other countries are creating internet enterprise zones specifically to attract this business.
What about the social benefits you mentioned? That seems like a stretch to say networks can replace government services.

I'm not saying replace, but networks can deliver remarkable efficiency. Kickstarter will provide more arts funding this year than the National Endowment. In the UK, Funding Circle created a whole new source of small business capital when banks weren't lending. We're going to have to do more with less anyway - networks can help us optimize scarce resources instead of fighting over them.
So what's your main ask here? What should policymakers actually do?

Defend the freedom to innovate. Keep the cost of innovation low, make sure financing stays available, keep markets accessible. We don't need R&D grants or industrial policy - we need policymakers to recognize when they're being asked to tilt the playing field toward incumbents. The next generation isn't asking us to solve their problems, they're asking us not to get in their way while they solve them themselves.

source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Brad Burnham's Keynote Speech for CDT's 2012 Annual Dinner.m4a_spaced_spaced.txt

messages.content: So you think government institutions are fundamentally broken because they're old bureaucracies that can't adapt to network technologies?

Not exactly broken - more like they're artifacts of a different era. Most of our institutions, both public and private, were built around hierarchical bureaucratic models that made sense 150 years ago. But we're seeing the emergence of network-based alternatives that organize people much more efficiently. The problem is these old bureaucratic structures are having trouble keeping up.
But networks can be manipulated too right? Like what stops someone from gaming Uber ratings or Yelp reviews?

That's actually less of a problem than you'd think. We've found with platforms like Yelp that organic ratings are pretty hard to spoof - fake reviews usually come from the same IP addresses and patterns that make them easy to spot and filter out. The real opportunity is using all that data these networks naturally produce. Uber knows pickup times, drop-off locations, driver ratings - information that would cost governments massive amounts to collect with traditional inspectors.
Ok but how do we regulate that data use? Seems like a privacy nightmare waiting to happen

Exactly the right question. Take Airbnb - if you're regulating that market, you need to figure out what degree that data should be personally identifiable versus aggregated and abstracted. These aren't simple technical problems, they're moral problems about balancing individual privacy rights against government's need to know. But the point is we have this incredible resource that could let us manage these markets much more efficiently in a socially responsible way.
You mentioned boom/bust cycles from the internet era - are we heading for another crash if we move too fast on gov tech?

Possibly, and that's something we need to be conscious of. In the 90s we got overly enthusiastic without proper infrastructure and crashed hard. But what emerged from that crash was a much more sustainable commitment to the internet's real capabilities. The key is not getting ahead of ourselves while also not losing sight of long-term goals if we have setbacks - like if network data inadvertently harms someone and causes a backlash.
So what's your actual solution here? Just experiment with lots of small pilots?

Rather than trying to define perfect solutions through protracted political processes, yes - run lots of small experiments and learn from them. It's like trying to define tech standards - really hard to do top-down, but things that emerge organically often become de facto standards. Better to move forward with multiple approaches than try to get everyone on the same page first.
What about just using existing consumer tech? Why reinvent the wheel for government?

Exactly! I see NYC cops using smartphones more than their radios now. They just got an app that gives them access to all building department and housing authority data - something that would have cost $30-50 million as a government contract. The flow has reversed from the old NASA trickle-down model. Now it's civilians first, then military and government. Consumer market moves faster because there's bigger markets and open source tools that let people build quickly.
But government procurement is still stuck in the stone age right?

Right, that's the real bottleneck. The technology exists, but government is constrained by purchasing contract law and bureaucratic processes while the private consumer market races ahead. We need to break those barriers down and find ways to embrace consumer services. The hard part isn't making the tech work - it's working through the privacy, security, and safety implications when you introduce these tools into government.

Brad, I've been thinking about your platform consolidation argument. You say Microsoft was actually good for consumers because it standardized interfaces, but bad for innovation. Isn't that a bit contradictory? How can something be simultaneously good and bad?

It's paradoxical but true. Look at what happened with Windows - Microsoft had the luxury of watching all the innovation happening on their platform, then deciding what to pull down into the OS or Office suite. It killed innovation at the application layer, but it did standardize user experience and made computing more accessible to regular people. Same pattern we're seeing now with web platforms.
OK but you're basically saying we need to break up big tech then? That seems like a pretty extreme solution just to get more developer innovation.

I'm not calling for breakups - I'm saying entrepreneurs will find another way. Think about Twitter's early days with the open API - we had TweetDeck, all these creative apps building on top. Then Twitter realized they needed advertising revenue and had to control content display, so they cut off all those developers. Now no smart developer builds on closed platforms after getting burned so many times.
So your solution is crypto protocols? That seems like a pretty big leap. Most crypto projects have been disasters - you even mentioned 80% of ICOs went dark.

The ICO bubble was inevitable - you had early stage teams with no experience building distributed systems, raising money at inflated prices when the infrastructure couldn't even support them at scale. It was like 1999 IPOs happening in 1993 before we had browsers. But the core insight remains: developers want platforms that can't be evil, that can't pull the rug out from under them.
Can't be evil is a nice slogan but how does that actually work in practice? Someone still has to make decisions about protocol changes, governance, etc.

That's the beauty of the architecture - governance can be relatively limited when the system is trustable by design. Bitcoin is the proof point - we're in year 10 and it's still solid, hasn't been hacked. There's forking as a governance mechanism, though it's painful. People are working on more elegant approaches, but the ideal is minimal governance because you can trust the underlying architecture.

But what about the business model problem? You keep talking about platforms taking too much rake - Uber, Airbnb taking their cut. How exactly does a crypto protocol solve that without just creating a different set of rent extractors?

Here's what's fascinating - imagine a ride sharing protocol where drivers have tokens in their cash register and passengers have them in their wallet. To participate, you need to own some currency. As the network grows, the people actually creating and consuming value - drivers and riders - participate in the appreciation. They're not just getting squeezed by shareholders looking for a 3x return. The value creators become the value capturers.

That sounds pretty theoretical though. Any actual examples of this working at scale? Because most crypto projects I see are either pure speculation or trying to solve problems that don't really exist.

You're right to be skeptical - we're very early. I'm still in the intuition phase, not conviction yet. But think about the alternative: continued consolidation, dominant players limiting innovation, fewer investment opportunities. The current trajectory isn't pretty. When I see developers who've been burned by platforms multiple times looking for something they can trust, that tells me there's real demand for this model, even if the execution is still being figured out.
I read your interview about USV's early days. You mentioned that you and Fred come at opportunities from different angles - theory vs practice. Can you explain what you mean by that?

Fred has this way of starting from practical reality and working toward an opportunity. Like when he was a teenager imagining all records on one hard drive - very tangible, immediate problem he wanted solved. I tend to start from theoretical frameworks and work down. I was studying political theory in college, thinking about power and information flows, then translating that into how information impacts markets. When we meet in the middle on something, we're usually right because we've stress-tested it from both directions.
But didn't that theoretical approach slow you down? Fred wanted to just start investing in 'the internet' and you made him spend months working through frameworks

It did slow us down, and Fred was definitely impatient with it. But I wasn't comfortable pitching investors without knowing exactly what we were going to do. We ended up finding Carlotta Perez's work on technological cycles, which helped us understand we were at the right moment - moving from infrastructure phase to applications layer. That theoretical grounding was crucial because it gave us conviction when everyone else had written off internet investing after the crash. If we'd been in a consolidation period, my approach wouldn't have worked. But the timing was right for taking that pause.
You got 80+ rejections raising that first fund. Did you ever think maybe the market was telling you something?

Never about the thesis itself. We'd both invested in internet companies successfully before the crash. We knew this was fundamentally transformative infrastructure. What I learned was that LPs fund investors, not just ideas. We were a first-time fund trying to raise right when LPs had been burned by a bunch of first-time internet funds that collapsed post-bubble. The timing was working against us from a fundraising perspective, even though it was perfect from an investing perspective. The rejection was about our profile as fund managers, not about whether the internet had a future.
OK but then when you started investing, how did you know what to look for? Web 2.0 wasn't even a term yet

That's exactly right - we were investing in things before we had names for them. Delicious was this perfect example. Josh built a bookmarking service, but it wasn't just about bookmarks - it was social, it was in the cloud, people could follow tags and discover through other people's interests. We were all in on it because it validated these emerging patterns we were seeing, but we didn't call it 'Web 2.0' or 'social' yet. The thesis kept developing as we invested. Having that theoretical framework helped us recognize the patterns even when we couldn't name them.
Interesting about the naming thing. Was Fred's blog part of figuring this out? Seems risky to give away your best ideas

The blog was hugely important, though our LPs thought Fred was crazy. They'd ask if he actually did any work since he was spending time writing and giving away ideas. But we both intuitively believed in it. It's that Bill Joy principle - there are more smart people outside your company than inside. The blog created this dialogue with the marketplace that helped us find those people and refine our thinking. Many of the ideas Fred wrote about came from our conversations, then got developed further through reader feedback. It was maybe the first real VC blog, and now you can't be in the business without that kind of marketplace dialogue.
The Twitter story is fascinating. You basically interviewed them instead of getting pitched. But you still seem to have regrets about how it turned out?

The Twitter investment was phenomenal for returns, but yes, I have regrets about what it could have been. We knew Twitter was essentially a protocol. If we'd had conviction they could monetize as a protocol rather than as a closed platform, and really pushed for that at the board level, Twitter today could look more like Ethereum - worth 10x what Elon paid for it. But the timing was wrong. When Bill Gross started buying up Twitter clients and accumulating 20-30% of users, it felt existential. We didn't have frameworks for protocol tokens or shared incentive structures yet. We were about five years too early for that thinking.
So you think there's still an opportunity for someone to build the 'protocol version' of Twitter?

Absolutely. Maybe it's still an open opportunity, maybe someone else's, maybe even ours. The difference between Twitter as a protocol versus Twitter as a media megaphone owned by one person - that's not just about money, though Ethereum being worth 450 billion versus Twitter selling for 45 billion tells you something. It's about what kind of tool it could be for the world. The infrastructure and understanding of how to monetize protocols exists now in ways it didn't in 2010-2011. Someone just needs to execute on it.
I read your interview about USV's evolution. You mentioned Bitcoin as a protocol rather than just internet money - what made that framing so important for your investment approach?

That protocol lens was crucial because it shifted how we thought about what we were investing in. Instead of seeing Bitcoin as just digital currency, we recognized it as computing infrastructure - like HTTP or SMTP but with the ability to store data and capture value. That opened up our thinking to invest in things like Filecoin for storage-as-a-protocol and identity projects like what became Stacks. We weren't just betting on money, we were betting on a new class of programmable infrastructure.
But you also admitted you were too early on some of those protocol bets. What was missing?

We needed Ethereum and smart contracts to emerge first. We had the right intuition about protocols, but we were investing before there was a programmable substrate that developers could actually build on top of. It's like trying to build web applications before the web browser existed. The infrastructure had to mature before our thesis could really play out.
You seem pretty concerned about consolidation - first with Web2 giants, now potentially with AI. Isn't this just how technology markets naturally evolve?

The difference is between consolidation through network effects versus consolidation that locks in structural openness. With Web2, we didn't fully understand how powerful those network effects would become until Google, Apple, Facebook and Amazon had already built their moats. Now with AI requiring billions in capital for model training, we're seeing similar dynamics. But the question is whether we can find mechanisms that 'can't be evil' - not just promise not to be evil, but are structurally unable to do rug pulls on everyone building on top.
You have this theory that AI might actually break down platform silos. Seems optimistic given how entrenched these companies are.

Think about it practically - users are getting comfortable telling AIs to do work that's too tedious for them. Why shouldn't I be able to tell an AI to recreate my SoundCloud playlist in Spotify? As people realize AIs can break down these artificial barriers between services, they'll start demanding it. The companies whose business models depend on keeping users trapped in silos will have to rethink their approach. It's bottom-up pressure, not top-down regulation.
But you're betting on decentralized AI infrastructure too. How does that actually compete with the scale advantages of big tech?

The same way Bitcoin mining works - you get a million people to turn on their computers and collectively do something that no single entity could do alone. Decades of software development in decentralized computing makes this possible now. Combined with crypto's ability to create incentive structures and global instant payments, you can bootstrap networks that don't require billions in upfront capital. The infrastructure becomes shared rather than owned by one player.
After 20+ years doing this, what keeps you engaged? Seems like you could have retired comfortably by now.

It's the learning environment. When entrepreneurs come in and I can interrogate them about domains they've been thinking deeply about, I can zero in on the one insight that really matters instead of reading through pages of stuff I already know. That moment when you suddenly understand what they're building - that doesn't get old. Plus I get to do it alongside curious people at USV without taking on a lot of operational to-dos. It's an incredible privilege to sit with smart people and talk about interesting things all day.
So you're saying blockchain tech could break up the big tech monopolies? That seems like a huge leap from Bitcoin mining concerns

Think about it like the shift from telecom monopolies to TCP/IP. When you had centralized phone systems, you needed Ma Bell to connect calls. But once you put networking capability on every host, you didn't need the intermediary anymore. Same principle could apply to data - if personal data moves from centralized stores to protocols, you break the network effects that keep these platforms dominant.
But TCP/IP was incredibly simple - send/receive. What you're describing sounds way more complex with consensus mechanisms, governance, crypto...

Exactly right - and that's the danger. We could kill this thing by trying to solve too big a problem too early. TCP/IP worked because it just did send/receive, then you moved up one layer to storage. If we can just enable a data layer - store/don't store - without trying to prescriptively define everything above it, we might get that positive market dynamic without having to engineer the whole stack.
What about the quantum computing problem though? If quantum breaks all current crypto, doesn't that undermine the whole trust model?

That's actually the perfect example of why governance matters more than the tech problem itself. When quantum hits, we'll need to migrate to quantum-resistant encryption. Look at what happened with the DAO hack - Ethereum forked to reverse the theft, but Ethereum Classic stayed with 'code is law.' The market voted: the fork has 10x the market cap. The real question isn't technical, it's who decides how we upgrade - democracy of coin holders? A benevolent dictator like Linus? Something in between?
So you're basically saying Elizabeth Warren should love blockchain because it's anti-monopoly, even though it looks anarchist and unregulated?

That's the trick, isn't it? Jason Furman said the biggest drag on growth is market concentration - that's extraordinary coming from the Chair of the National Economic Council. Blockchain could be the tool to actually open up markets instead of just talking about breaking up big tech. But yeah, we need to get policymakers to see past the scary anarchist crypto-libertarian surface and recognize it as their friend in fighting concentration.
Seems like a hard sell when you're asking people to trust math they don't understand for systems that need to last decades

Right, and that's where we get into the fundamental question of how humans think about memory and record keeping. We went from proving land ownership by getting villagers to remember transactions in the 11th century, all the way to self-authenticating databases protected by encryption. The paradigm shift isn't just technical - it's about whether we're willing to trust large numbers being hard to factor in polynomial time. My anarchist cypherpunk friends are already trying to go post-quantum because they're not so sure about that math anymore.
Do you really think we can build software systems that'll work reliably for 50-70 years? Seems optimistic

It's a great question, and honestly, I think it requires that these systems be living things that can evolve. They can't be owned or controlled by any one entity - they have to be community-shared assets that we all collaborate on building. When certificates expire, when crypto needs upgrading, when quantum computing arrives, you need all the smart people working together in the open. As Bill Joy said, no matter how many smart people you employ, there are always more working for somebody else.

source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Panel Discussion_ _Blockchain and FOSS, Problems and Possibilities_ (SFLC 2016).m4a_spaced2.txt

messages.content: Brad, I've been thinking about your blockchain thesis. You compare it to TCP/IP disrupting telecom monopolies, but isn't that a bit grandiose? Most blockchain applications seem to be about making existing processes more efficient, not fundamentally restructuring markets.

I think you're looking at the obvious use cases - the efficiency plays in banking back offices and supply chains. Those are real and valuable. But the more profound opportunity is what I call the re-decentralization of the web. Right now we live under GAFA - Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon - because they control centralized databases of user behavior. Blockchain enables something fundamentally different: users controlling comprehensive data stores about themselves, with services coming to them on a permissioned basis rather than the other way around.
That sounds nice in theory, but network effects are powerful for a reason. Why wouldn't we just end up with a few dominant blockchain platforms that recreate the same centralization problem?

You're absolutely right that network effects will concentrate things - we're already seeing it with Bitcoin at $10 billion market cap, Ethereum at $1 billion, then a steep drop-off. But I think we'll get an inverted pyramid structure: a small number of robust, well-tested base blockchains that have survived years of hacking attempts, with services built on top rather than spinning up new chains. The key difference is that the data isn't trapped in one company's silo - it's distributed.

But here's what I don't get about the token economics. You mentioned this tension between equity value and token value - that they might be inversely related. How does that actually work as a business model?

It's a completely new phenomenon - the first time open source has had its own business model. Say you create a decentralized storage system where people earn tokens for providing storage capacity. If the company hoards too many tokens to create shareholder value, participants won't want to contribute because they're just enriching the equity holders. But if the company gives away too many tokens, there's no equity value. It's this delicate balance, and honestly, the tokens might end up being significantly more valuable than the equity in many cases.
That seems unsustainable though. What prevents this from becoming another speculative bubble like we saw with synthetic derivatives?

Look, I'm not going to pretend there aren't risks. The token dynamics can get pretty wild - we've got systems where the token represents both the utility within the network and speculative value based on usage growth. There's definitely bubble potential. But the underlying infrastructure being built is real. The question is whether we can thread the needle between speculation and building actual utility. The key is finding that Goldilocks balance between proof of work and proof of stake to achieve equilibrium.
Speaking of risks, what about security? If everything moves to blockchain infrastructure, doesn't that create a huge target for nation-states or sophisticated attackers?

That's the double-edged sword. Right now we have proprietary systems that are all different - harder to attack at scale. Move everything to blockchain and yes, you create attractive targets. But you also get resilience through distribution. Look at voting systems - if someone compromises one database, the other nodes say 'no, that didn't happen.' The question is whether we're better off with distributed trust or security through obscurity. I lean toward distributed, but we need to be deliberate about how we build this out.
This all assumes people actually want to control their own data. Most users seem happy to trade privacy for convenience. Why would they suddenly start managing their own comprehensive data stores?

You're right that user behavior today suggests otherwise. But I think we're at an inflection point. When users had to go to 20-30 different sites that each had incomplete views of them, convenience trumped control. But imagine flipping that - one comprehensive data store that you control, with 20-30 services coming to you with specific permission requests. You get better service because they see the full picture, but you maintain control. The convenience equation changes when the infrastructure enables it.
Alright, but let's get practical. What's your timeline for this playing out? Are we talking 5 years, 20 years?

The efficiency plays are happening now - banks are already implementing private blockchains for back office operations. The re-decentralization piece is longer term. We need those robust base layer protocols first, then the applications layer. I'd say we're in the early infrastructure building phase now, mainstream applications probably 5-7 years out, and the full market structure changes maybe 10-15 years. But remember, Snapchat is over five years old and was the last major consumer web service to reach global scale. The centralization problem is getting worse, which creates more pressure for alternatives.

source: /workspace/pollux/datasets/raw/brad/corpus/transcript-Panel Discussion_ _Blockchain and FOSS, Problems and Possibilities_ (SFLC 2016).m4a_spaced1.txt

messages.content: So you're saying blockchain tokens could basically replace Uber's equity model? How does that actually work in practice?

Think about what Uber really does - it coordinates drivers and passengers using GPS, maps, reputation systems. Those were brilliant innovations but they haven't changed much since launch. The problem is Uber's $60-70 billion valuation creates an expectation of $10 billion in earnings, which means they have to extract more value from drivers and passengers over time to satisfy equity shareholders. But if you reduce that platform to a protocol with tokens incentivizing the economic behavior - getting drivers to show up, giving passengers confidence - then as the network grows, the token appreciates in both the driver's cash register and passenger's wallet. They participate in the value they're creating instead of having it extracted by shareholders.
But tokens are incredibly volatile right now. How do you ask someone living paycheck to paycheck to subject their income to that kind of risk?

You're absolutely right - that's the biggest practical challenge we're facing. We're seeing experiments with long lockups that make it harder to get in and out of tokens, requirements to bond or stake to participate which dampens volatility. Some people are working on fiat-pegged cryptocurrencies that are more stable for actual exchange. Honestly, we don't have a complete answer yet, but we have a lot of experiments happening. The hope is we're lurching toward something more stable and trusted. If you're building something that doesn't require significant upfront capital investment, you can design tokens to be less attractive to speculators.
What about this reputation/identity thing on blockchain? Sounds pretty dystopian to have an immutable record of everything you've ever done

I get why that sounds scary, but think about it differently. I live in NYC where there's anonymity, but I also have a cottage in a tiny community of 250 people where I've been all my life. In that small place, there's no anonymity - everyone knows your history. The cost is you can't escape who you are, but the value is enormous: you can accomplish a lot because you've built trust over decades and everyone knows you've never screwed anyone over. An immutable blockchain record could work similarly - if you control the keys and can parcel out pieces of your interaction history as needed, you could build verifiable reputation that serves you, not some service provider. The cryptography has to be trustworthy, but if it is, voluntary reputation building could be incredibly powerful.
But what about the right to be forgotten? People change, make mistakes when young...

That's the real trade-off, and I think it comes down to individual choice and community standards. You should be able to choose whether to use your reputation to achieve something - which might require revealing your history - or go to the big city and be anonymous, though then you lose the leverage that reputation provides. The key question is who gets to make these choices. Some acts a community just can't 'unsee' - there are things that matter for trust and safety. But these should be decisions made by individuals and communities, not imposed top-down. It's really about designing the metagame - the rules about who gets to make the rules.
You mentioned networks of networks for currencies. How would that actually work for cooperatives?

Picture it like the early internet - a network of networks where you could be part of a smaller network but still interact with the larger one. Instead of governments backing currencies, you'd have collectives of community members or co-ops saying 'we'll accept this medium of exchange.' The really interesting part would be watching exchange rates between different networks and making those exchanges highly automated and seamless. Maybe just a chip in your phone that handles the conversion behind the scenes. If that exchange can be really smooth, you could bootstrap a network of networks where smaller cooperatives accept these various currencies. Co-ops have always struggled with scale - this could be a way to stay small and local while still participating in larger economic networks.
Are you worried about the dystopian possibilities? Like China's social credit system on blockchain?

Absolutely. I was completely enthralled with the early internet and naively believed open communication would make dictatorships impossible - the Arab Spring fueled that hope before it was crushed. Technology is morally agnostic, and dictators figured out how to use it more efficiently than we did. China's announced social credit system, Modi cracking down on cash - there's no better tool for that control than blockchain. This time I'm going in as a believer in decentralized innovation and open public data stores, but much less sanguine about where it leads. The only advantage is the cycle between Web 2 and Web 3 is close enough that we remember getting hoodwinked last time. We need to rapidly accelerate building competing systems because you can't stop others from creating systems with different intent.
So you're saying networks are fundamentally disrupting traditional business models - but isn't this just the usual creative destruction? What makes this different from past technological shifts?

The key difference is the layered architecture of the internet. For the first time in 200 years, we're bypassing the bureaucratic hierarchy model entirely. When Lending Club connects borrowers directly to lenders, they're cutting out the entire banking infrastructure that used to be necessary. The internet separates communications from applications - so now any entrepreneur can reach Bangladesh without building their own infrastructure. That's fundamentally different from previous disruptions.
OK but if these network effects are so powerful and inevitable, why are you worried about regulation killing them? Shouldn't they just win anyway?

They will ultimately win - it's like grass growing through pavement. But here's the problem: when incumbents use regulation to resist these new models, they're not preventing the disruption, they're making it much more catastrophic when it finally happens. Instead of lots of small, graceful transitions, you get massive collapses like the financial crisis. The hotel industry literally wrote the language that New York used to ban temporary hotels, targeting Airbnb without even mentioning them by name.
That's pretty concerning. But you also say startups need to recognize there IS a role for government - isn't that contradictory? How do you thread that needle?

Not contradictory at all. Look, there will be unscrupulous landlords on Airbnb and nasty drivers on Uber. A hacker in Russia can steal from your mother across the globe. We can't just pretend problems don't exist. The key is recognizing that regulators should think of the internet as the solution, not the problem. Take Uber - it generates massive amounts of data that regulators have never had access to before. Every pickup location, route, payment, rating. That's a regulatory goldmine if they work WITH the network instead of trying to shove it into old regulatory boxes.
This "Regulation 2.0" idea is interesting. Can you give me a concrete example of how this would work differently?

Sure. Right now NYC regulates taxis by putting inspectors in unmarked cars to flag violations - incredibly expensive and inefficient. But with Uber's network, regulators could have real-time data on every ride: where, when, how long, how much, plus mutual ratings. If regulators recognize these platforms as intermediaries rather than taxi companies, and give them some liability protection in exchange for data sharing, they could regulate the edges of the network much more effectively. Instead of chasing cars around Manhattan, they'd have a complete picture of what's actually happening.
You mention startups being historically allergic to politics but now needing to engage. What does that actually look like in practice?

We've proven we can stop bad legislation - when there's dramatic overreach, the internet community rises up and says no, and it's shockingly effective. But we haven't figured out how to advocate FOR things, which is much harder. When you're building switches and routers, making things 10x faster is apolitical. But when you challenge financial services incumbents, people care immediately. The problem is that incumbents have existing cash flows to influence policy, while startups don't even know they need to be politically aware until it's too late.
Why Iceland specifically though? Seems like you're suggesting they could be some kind of regulatory pioneer?

Exactly. Iceland has unique advantages - naturally entrepreneurial, you can reach global markets from this isolated edge of the world, plus energy and data center benefits. But the real opportunity is the homogeneous population with a tractable political process. Iceland could build a global brand around internet-friendly regulation, defining things like user rights in cloud data that are completely confused in the US and Europe. It's not about pushing some libertarian free-for-all agenda - it's about asking what constraints need to be released to unleash innovation and economic development on this platform.
That sounds politically risky though. How do you avoid looking like you're just trying to be a tax haven for tech companies?

You're absolutely right - push too far and you look like you're just about libertarian free stuff with no real economic development message. The key is framing it around future versus past, not left versus right. I work with both sides in the US on these issues. The opportunity is for a political party to seize that vision of the future and bring along young people who grew up on the internet and understand its value. But it will be tricky - you want to release constraints for innovation, not just create a regulatory Wild West.
